youtube_caption: let's make easy baked pears for the topping combine 1 4 cup of rolled oats a quarter cup of chopped pecans 1 4 cup of chopped dried cranberries a quarter cup of cinnamon 2 tablespoons of maple syrup and two tablespoons of melted butter stir that together and set it aside you'll need four pairs for this recipe cut them in half then scoop out the centers make a small slice in the back of each pair this will keep the pears from wobbling in your baking dish now divide the filling evenly between each pair last but not least sprinkle a little bit of cinnamon over the top then bake in a preheated oven uncovered at 375 degrees fahrenheit for about 25 to 30 minutes depending on the firmness of your pears these are great served as a dessert or you can put them over your morning yogurt
Ground_Truth: Let's make easy baked pears (upbeat music). For the topping, combine 1/4 cup of rolled oats, a quarter cup of chopped pecans, 1/4 cup of chopped dried cranberries, a quarter cup of cinnamon, two tablespoons of maple syrup, and two tablespoons of melted butter. Stir that together and set it aside. You'll need four pears for this recipe. Cut them in half, then scoop out the centers, make a small slice in the back of each pear this will keep the pears from wobbling in your baking dish. Now, divide the filling evenly between each pear. Last but not least, sprinkle a little bit of cinnamon over the top. Then bake in a preheated oven, uncovered, at 375 degrees Fahrenheit for about 25 to 30 minutes, depending on the firmness of your pears. These are great served as a dessert, or you can put them over your morning yogurt (upbeat music) (fork clunks).
Chatgpt_caption: Let's make easy baked pears. For the topping, combine 1/4 cup of rolled oats, 1/4 cup of chopped pecans, 1/4 cup of chopped dried cranberries, 1/4 teaspoon of cinnamon, 2 tablespoons of maple syrup, and 2 tablespoons of melted butter. Stir that together and set it aside. You'll need four pears for this recipe. Cut them in half, then scoop out the centers. Make a small slice in the back of each pear; this will keep the pears from wobbling in your baking dish. Now, divide the filling evenly between each pear. Last but not least, sprinkle a little bit of cinnamon over the top. Then bake in a preheated oven, uncovered, at 375 degrees Fahrenheit for about 25 to 30 minutes, depending on the firmness of your pears. These are great served as a dessert, or you can put them over your morning yogurt.

youtube_caption: homemade pizza dough no special equipment needed warm water active dry yeast sugar and let it sit for five minutes if the yeast doesn't wake up start over you should be able to see it argue back in real time add in bread flour olive oil osmo salt give it a little mixy mix if it's too sticky add a little flour and you want to work the dough until you can almost see through it without it tearing with no equipment you have to be patient it takes 15-20 minutes divide this into four now you can cover this let it rise on the countertop for two hours or cover them and put them in the fridge for 24 hours you could actually leave them for up to a week this is adam rogocia's pizza dough recipe it's not mine i found it to be the easiest best pizza dough recipe and if you don't approve and you don't think this is authentic don't argue with us argue with your mama there you go it poofed up nice and beautiful
Ground_Truth: Homemade pizza dough, no special equipment needed. Warm water, active dry yeast, sugar and let it sit for five minutes. If the yeast doesn't wake up, start over. You should be able to see it bubble back in real time. Add in Bread flour, Olive oil, Osmo salt. Give it a little mixy-mix. If it's too sticky, add a little flour. And you want to work the dough until you can almost see through it without it tearing. With no equipment, you have to be patient. It takes 15-20 minutes. Divide this into four. Now, you can cover this, let it rise on the countertop for two hours, or cover them and put them in the fridge for 24 hours. You could actually leave them for up to a week. This is Adam Rogocia's pizza dough recipe. It's not mine. I found it to be the easiest, best pizza dough recipe. If you don't approve and you don't think this is authentic, don't argue with us; argue with your mama. There you go; it poofed up nice and beautiful.

Ground_Truth: When you're in the mood for something sweet and you're looking for something on the lighter side, this peanut butter banana smoothie is for you. It's naturally sweetened with fruit and reminds me of a milkshake, but it's completely dairy-free and it will leave you feel satisfied for hours. To get started, you'll need to make sure that you have frozen bananas on hand. I like to peel mine and store them whole in an airtight container with a lid, so they are easy to grab later. Don't forget to peel the bananas before you freeze them, or the peel will be very difficult to remove later. These should be frozen solid in 6 to 8 hours, but I always like to keep a container like this in my freezer so I can easily make smoothies or banana ice cream anytime. To make the peanut butter banana smoothie, you'll need 1/2 cup of milk or waterâI'm using almond milk to keep it dairy-free. Next, youâll add in 1 frozen banana, and I'm going to break this into a few pieces so it's easier for the blender to break down. If you don't have a high-speed blender, you can cut the banana into small coin shapes, and that will make them even easier for your blender to break down smoothly. Add in 2 tablespoons of all-natural peanut butter, 1 tablespoon of ground flax seeds to help keep you full, and 1/2 teaspoon of vanilla extract to make this taste like a milkshake. This makes a single serving, but you can easily double the ingredients to make enough to share. Blend everything together and stop to scrape down the sides as needed. This recipe doesn't use a lot of liquid to help keep the smoothie thick, but you can always add an extra splash of almond milk or water if you need to keep things blending smoothly. At this point, you can taste the smoothie and add any sweetener if you need it. The riper the bananas you use, the sweeter the smoothie will be, but sometimes I add a little honey to help boost the overall flavor. You can also add a handful of ice if you'd like a thicker consistency. Serve this peanut butter banana smoothie right away for the best taste and texture. It really tastes like a healthy dessert, so it's a great way to satisfy your sweet tooth. If you have any leftovers, I like to pour this smoothie into an ice pop mold and freeze it overnight for a cold treat later. For even more quick and easy real food recipes, be sure to check out my other videos here, and you'll find even more delicious smoothie ideas over on my website Detoxinista.com.

{"title": "My Vegan 'Honey' Mustard Salad with CRISPY TOFU!", "description": "If you hate salads, this recipe will change your mind! It's plant-based, easy and so delicious. And you can use this crispy tofu in a variety of recipes!
🌱 Recipe eBooks: http://payhip.com/tessbegg
✨ Follow me on Instagram: http://instagram.com/tessbegg
✉️ Contact: hello@tessbegg.com
🦋 Buy my clothes on my Depop: https://www.depop.com/tessbegg/

*Discount Codes:*
🥥 Use my code 'TES0712' for a discount on your next iHerb order. I shop on iHerb regularly for products that I can't find in-store here or can find cheaper on there (vanilla, choc chips, GF flours, supplements) https://bit.ly/tessiherb
🥝 Complement Supplements discount code 'TESS10' http://bit.ly/tesscomplement
🍉 Use 'tess25' to get $25 OFF the MOD Cold Press Juicer I use: https://bit.ly/tessmod

*Products I use:*
Vitamix blender: https://amzn.to/3VjCrnj
Plant-Based Book: https://amzn.to/3AnxQXv
Coffee Machine: https://amzn.to/444jB7E
Microphone: https://amzn.to/41Sswaa

*This Recipe:* 
[Serves 2]
Crispy tofu:
200g firm organic tofu
1 cup bread crumbs of choice (I used panko)
1/4 cup flour of choice
1/3 cup water or soy milk
Pinch of salt, pepper
Enough cooking oil to shallow fry

‘Honey’ mustard dressing:
2 tbsp Dijon mustard
2 tbsp maple syrup
1/2 lemon juiced
1/4 tsp garlic powder
2 tbsp olive oil or hemp oil
Pinch of salt and pepper

Salad:
Lettuce
Cherry tomatoes
Red onion
Avocado

1. Firmly press the tofu to remove any water and slice into 1 cm thick rectangle pieces and set aside. In a shallow bowl combine the flour, water, salt and pepper, and in a separate bowl add the bread crumbs.
2. Dip the tofu into the batter and drip any excess off, then toss it in the panko crumbs and place it on a plate.
3. Place a frypan over medium heat and add enough cooking oil to cook 1/2 of the tofu. Once the oil is hot add in the tofu pieces and cook until lightly golden on each side. Be careful to not burn the tofu. Once ready set aside on a paper towel to remove any excess oil.
4. In a small bowl whisk together the dressing ingredients.
5. Cut the tofu into smaller pieces. Add all the salad ingredients to a bowl and pour over the dressing.

🥑 What I Eat In A Day: https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PLorYSJ4NI-caTv1pd9kkGbt8s20H5V5cY
🧘🏼‍♀️ Workouts: https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PLorYSJ4NI-cbJAkU3EYOMRdNTxo77G7Ug

Ground_Truth: Thereâs nothing worse than a boring salad, so today I'm going to show you how to make the most delicious crispy tofu and honey mustard salad. It's plant-based, full of so much flavor, and you're going to love it. Let's get straight into the recipe. I am going to start by pressing firm tofu to remove the excess water, then slicing it into one-centimeter-thick rectangles. I am going to add the breadcrumbs in one bowl, and in the separate bowl, I'm adding the flour, salt, and pepper, mix that until smooth. This will be the batter. Next, add the tofu to the batter and drip off any excess, then toss it in the breadcrumbs until fully coated. Set it aside and repeat. Next, heat cooking oil in a pan over medium-high heat. Once it's hot, add the tofu and cook each side until golden. Then, remove the tofu and set it aside on a paper towel to soak up any excess oil. Once it's cooled, you can cut it into cubes. For the dressing, I'm using Dijon mustard, maple syrup, lemon juice, olive oil, garlic powder, salt, and pepper, and mixing it together. Honey mustard dressing was one of my favorites before I went vegan, and it's super easy to make plant-based by just swapping out the honey for maple syrup. I am sure, you can also use agave or other sweeteners. Next, I'll cut up some fresh lettuce, red onion, cherry tomatoes, and avocado. Look how perfect that is! Then, I'll assemble that in a bowl, add on the tofu, and pour over that delicious dressing. The salad is ready, and it looks so good. You can definitely bulk this up by adding some quinoa or roasted sweet potatoes. I like it basic and simple because sometimes the best salads are the ones that don't have a million ingredients in them. Now for the taste test. That is so good! I am going to pop the recipe in the description of this video so you can check it out. Be sure to let me know what you think in the comments, give this video a thumbs up, and don't forget to subscribe. I'll see you all in my next video. Thanks for watching. Bye! [Music]

{"title": "Why Katana are Made from Crappy Steel #Shorts", "description": "I question I often get from everyone is, “Why do you still use crappy steel (Tamahagane) to make katana in Japan?” I had an opportunity to ask actual katana swordsmiths this question, 
and they told me these three reasons.

1. It wouldn’t be a katana
2. It’s high-quality carbon steel
3. Artistic coincidence

First of all, blades that are not made from Tamahagane can not be called a katana in Japan. In Japan, katana are defined as "works of art," so traditional materials and methods of making them are required.

Next, Tamahagane is not the best kind of steel, but as a carbon steel, it actually has a good quality. And carbon steel is a very convenient steel for craftsmen who make everything by hand, because it's easy to work with.

Finally, it is only because craftsmen make katana by hand using Tamahagane, which is not perfect steel, that artistic coincidence is possible. If katana were a uniform tool mass-produced in a factory, it would not be called a work of art. It is precisely because katana are believed to be the home of the gods that it is meaningful for craftsmen to collaborate with nature in their creation.

If you’d like to learn more about Japanese traditional culture, Kyoto, and social problems in Japan, please check out my channel & subscribe!

*The content is based on personal studies and experience
There is no intention of denying other theories and cultural aspects

I think you've done a much better job than most in sort of leveraging your existing work/research for creating/editing for shorts.  Great work Shogo!"}, {"comment": "I can tell you... in one word! "TRADITION!""}, {"comment": "Honestly, the fact that they turned iron rich sands into anything is basically a miracle. There is a LOT of work that goes into turning the byproducts of smelting into usable steel. In some cases up to roughly 90% of the material is taken away in the process, most of which is slag and impurities. European finery forges did much the same thing. It's remarkable to see the convergent design from the other side of the world."}, {"comment": "I would say, reason #4: Nobody uses an Katana as a weapon nowadays, just as an piece of art or for Iaido. So it would make no sense to change an tradition just for a better quality that is not needed."}, {"comment": "Same with clay :-) There are lots of different clays in Japan, but real artists use it “nama” with its little stones and chips of rottet plants. It’s these impurities that if you are lucky create a “happy mess” like “ishihaze” (stone explosion) in the glaze. Some glazes like shino are not even possible without nama tsuchi. It is really beautiful to see it in forging as well, thank you!"}, {"comment": "In regards for the „not real katana“ point: I am aware of several Shinken wich were purposely forged with European steel in the Edo Period. I doubt the Blacksmiths and Samurai wielding them would agree at all that these swords are not real Katana."}, {"comment": "The government is why they are made from iron sand… basically. 

It comes down to the laws regarding sword making."}, {"comment": "They've actually done metallurgical analysis of swords from the Muromachi period, and found the steel to be roughly equivalent to modern 1055 carbon steel (if I'm remembering all the trace percentages correctly). It isn't the best steel in the world, but it's perfectly acceptable blade steel. Moreover, the form of the katana was optimized for the type of steel it was made from. The idea was to make the best possible sword from the available materials. The same steel would make a very poor rapier, but it makes a perfectly fine katana."}, {"comment": "I'll never know why people think it's a bad steel. It's almost the same thing European swords were made from"}, {"comment": "You missed the most basic and fundamental part: Japanese used dirty metal because that’s all they had. The constant hammering and folding was to remove the impurities in the metal."}, {"comment": "‘Still cuts through limbs and bodies’"}, {"comment": "Since they're classified as works of art, they can also be easier to own legally."}, {"comment": "People knowing nothing about a craft telling the craftsmen how they should do said craft will never not be annoying"}, {"comment": "Imma just point out that all steel has carbon in it, "carbon steel" means "carbon carbonated iron""}, {"comment": "The artistic and historical aspects make sense. Besides, we don't use katana for killing anymore, why does it need to made from weapons grade steel?"}, {"comment": "There is one more thing that you missed, but that's okay because the inventors of the process didn't really know it either.

It's really high carbon, which is normally a terrible material for anything, but in the lengthy forging process most of the carbon is burned away, so you end up with quality steel, even though you start with terrible steel.
If you started with quality steel, you'd end up with pure, soft, iron.
Which is actually what happens on the core of the blade."}]}

Ground_Truth: Structured Query Language (SQL), or SQL for short, is the gold standard language for communicating with relational database management systems. You've likely heard of databases like MySQL, PostgreSQL, Microsoft SQL Server, and Oracle, all of which are based on SQL with their own little variations. It was initially developed in the early '70s to manipulate and retrieve data from IBM's System R database. By 1986, it was standardized into the syntax that remains extremely popular in technical applications today. A relational database organizes data into tables, kind of like an Excel spreadsheet, where columns contain attributes or types of data, while each row represents an individual record or data point with its own unique ID, known as a primary key. We can establish relationships between data points by taking the unique ID from one row and storing it on a different row in a different table in a special column known as a foreign key. In the "teams" table, the team ID is the primary key, but in the "players" table, it's the foreign key. And what that data structure tells us is that a player belongs to one team while a team can have many players. What we've done here is structured our data in its smallest normal form to eliminate duplication and redundancy. Now, the role of SQL is not only to read, create, update, and delete data, but also to join data together based on the relationships embedded within it. The syntax is comprised of several key parts. If we zoom all the way out, we have a statement or a piece of code that does something like reads or writes to the database. Within a statement, we have a variety of keywords that can manipulate the database. "Select" can be used to make a query for the columns that you want from a table. The column and table names are known as identifiers, but we may not want every row in the table, so we filter the results with the "where" keyword to only include records that meet a certain condition. It's like looping over every row in the table and only returning rows in the query where the predicate evaluates to true. Then we might use the "join" keyword to connect data from an entirely different table by matching the primary key on this table to the foreign key on another table. What we've created is a collection of clauses that make up a full SQL statement. We can now go and execute this code on the hundreds of different databases that support SQL. This has been Structured Query Language in 100 seconds. Like and subscribe if you want to see a full SQL tutorial. Thanks for watching, and I will see you in the next one.

A relational database organizes data into tables, similar to an Excel spreadsheet, where columns contain attributes or types of data, and each row represents an individual record or data point with its unique ID, known as a primary key. Relationships between data points are established by taking the unique ID from one row and storing it in a different table's row in a special column known as a foreign key. This data structure indicates that a player belongs to one team, while a team can have many players. By structuring our data in its smallest normal form, we eliminate duplication and redundancy.  The role of SQL is not only to read, create, update, and delete data but also to join data based on embedded relationships. The syntax consists of several key parts. At the highest level, we have a statement or a piece of code that performs an action like reading or writing to the database. Within a statement, we use various keywords to manipulate the database. The "SELECT" keyword is used to query for specific columns from a table. The column and table names are known as identifiers. To filter results, we use the "WHERE" keyword to include only records that meet specific conditions. This is akin to looping over every row in the table and returning rows where the predicate is true.  The "JOIN" keyword is used to connect data from different tables by matching the primary key on one table to the foreign key on another. This collection of clauses forms a complete SQL statement that can be executed on the numerous databases supporting SQL.  This has been an explanation of Structured Query Language in 100 seconds. Like and subscribe if you want to see a full SQL tutorial. Thanks for watching, and I'll see you in the next one.

Firing 110 ballistic missiles directly to Israel will not go without consequences. We will respond in our time, in our place, in the way that we choose, but exactly what that means is not clear. The world awaits Israel's next move. Can it punish Iran for launching this barrage without plunging the region into a much bigger war? Standing next to this gives a very powerful sense of the threat posed by Iran's missile onslaught on Saturday, but also the threat to the region should more of these missiles end up flying around. If Israel's retaliation escalates the war into a much bigger conflict, the risk of that happening is very real. In Tehran, Israel's enemies were warning again: the smallest Israeli retaliation will be met with another show of force. If, for any reason, the Zionist regime makes the slightest malevolent move against our homeland, our individuals, or our interests, it will definitely face a resolute and hard response. Iran stands behind Hamas, behind Hezbollah, behind others, but we are determined to win there and defend ourselves in all arenas. There is jeopardy, but opportunity too for Israel, say observers, to build on the coalition of allies and neighbors that helped defend Israel that night. You know, the real question is: what kind of response does the most harm to Iran and most benefits Israel? What does that mean? I think it probably means don't hit back at Iran in Iran right away, when there's a risk that you'll destroy the coalition arrayed against Iran and you won't be able to do much damage. Instead of attacking Iran, Israel could do more of this: assassinating Iranian allies. Today it killed another Hezbollah commander in southern Lebanon. Israel was also drilling its forces south of the border, a frontline for any escalating conflict with Iran and its proxies. If it happens, a widening war would likely engulf the region far beyond Israel's borders.

Ground_Truth: An ABC exclusive report reveals that Israel had planned retaliation for the Iranian attack and backed out twice this week. Since the drone assault, there has been much deliberation regarding the Israeli response to the Iranian attack on Saturday night. The Israeli security cabinet met multiple times as IDF Chief of Staff Hery Halevi and top US officials confirmed that a response will come. The nature of the response was never confirmed, this amid immense international pressure to halt any response out of fear of a wider regional conflict. Now, sources told ABC that on two separate nights this week, Israel prepared and then aborted retaliatory strikes against Iran. Reports indicate that a retaliation won't take place until the Passover holiday had passed. In further news, sources are reporting that the Biden administration would approve a large-scale Rafa operation, a delicate matter that had been previously rejected by the US if Israel would avoid any retaliatory action toward Iran. Overnight, heavy bombardments occurred in southern Lebanon as Israel retaliated against Hezbollah's attack on Wednesday. Fourteen IDF soldiers and four civilians were injured when Hezbollah fired two UAV drones and two anti-tank missiles at and around a community center in Arab El Aram, a village in the northern Galil. MA confirmed that of the wounded, six were seriously wounded, two moderately wounded, and six more lightly injured. In response, IDF fighter jets struck Hezbollah air defense infrastructure north of the city of Balbec, the infamous Hezbollah stronghold almost 100 kilometers from the Israeli border. Moreover, an IAF aircraft eliminated two Hezbollah terrorists identified in the area. Twenty-eight Google employees have been fired after protesting a recent deal with Israel. In the tech giant's New York office, anti-Israel activists protested the $1.2 billion contract with the Israeli government by occupying the office in New York City and Sunnyvale for several hours on Tuesday. Protesters were arrested by New York police officers after refusing to leave Google Cloud CEO Thomas Kion's office for almost 10 hours. All involved protesters were arrested and put on administrative leave, according to a Google spokesperson. Google Vice President of Global Security Chris Rakau was quoted saying, "Behavior like this has no place in our workplace, and we will not tolerate it." Rakau added, "It clearly violates multiple policies that all employees must adhere to, including our code of conduct and policy on harassment, discrimination, retaliation, standards of conduct, and workplace concerns." Ground_Truth: Every thunderstorm begins with a single raindrop, but trillions can follow, and all that water rushes downhillâthe early makings of a disaster. And it doesn't need to be raining where you are; a street like this in a valley can funnel the water, creating a dangerous setup for a flash flood. Heavy rainfall is the main ingredient in flash flooding; other factors include building in a flood zone, improper drainage, and ground that's just too wet and can't absorb any more water. And this happens fast. Now, look at the street; the water is business-to-business here, wall to wall. Whoa, what's this guy doing? And this water is about six inches now, and it's enough to stall vehicles, even float vehicles in some cases. You're gonna lose control, and you don't want to be this guy. So, if you come to the street that's flooded, turn around, don't drown. More than half of the deaths from flooding each year occur in a vehicle, and the water is still rising here. In fact, it's enough to flow through much, anything away that's not bolted down. At two feet, it's gonna carry away even cars like this. Oh my gosh, look at this; this is an SUV, and the water is high enough now to carry SUVs, pickup trucks. And this water is well inside these businesses, and this is scary; this is bad. That's not good to see something like that; we're talking some immense destruction with the force of this water. It can carry debris for miles, entire trees, in fact. Water flowing at just ten miles an hour can produce the same force as winds blowing at 300 miles an hour. Look at this; this is a huge log coming down the road, and so scary; you never know what's even in the water, but you can see some of the larger objects, including a truck right here. Absolutely life-threatening situations they come with flash flooding, and unfortunately, more heavy rain events are occurring due to our warming climate. So, you are more likely to be caught in this situation. If you ever do get stuck in a flash flood, try to stay calm, get as high as possible, and make yourself visible while you wait for first responders. Heavy rain events are predicted to be 40% more common for parts of the country within this century. Just as quickly as the water came up, it goes down, leaving behind a muddy mess of debris and dangers like sharp objects. Hazardous waste is also a big concern and downed power lines. So, follow the forecast and remember when a road is flooded, always, always turn around, don't drown.

Ground_Truth: In a historic moment, as Fan Non Cognac becomes the first woman member from Nagaland to preside over the Rajya Sabha. She was nominated to the panel of Vice Chairpersons by the Chairman of Rajya Sabha, Jagdeep Dhankar, last week. President Droupadi Murmu, who completed one year in office on Tuesday, arrived in Bhubaneswar this evening on a three-day visit to her home state, Odisha. Governor Professor Ganeshi Lal, Chief Minister Naveen Patnaik, Chief Justice of Odisha High Court Justices Murlidhar, and senior state government officials welcomed the president at the airport. And several parts of Odisha experienced rainfall activities under the influence of low pressure. The meteorological department has issued an orange warning for four districts: Gajpati, Ganjam, Malkangiri, and Koraput. Yellow warning has been sounded in eight other districts. Investigation expedited in connection with JE Civil Main written exam question paper leak. Balasore SP informs OTV that the mastermind of the racket is from outside Odisha. Five teams sent outside the state to apprehend the mastermind and others involved. Meanwhile, some of the accused were taken to an undisclosed location for further questioning. After 84 days, the Manipur government has partially lifted the ban on the internet in the ethnic violence-hit state. The state government has considered the decision to lift suspension in the case of broadband service conditionally in a liberalized manner, subject to the fulfillment of 10 conditions. Mobile internet, however, is still banned. [Music]

youtube_caption: three things to do for your first time in Seoul South Korea if you want an all-in-one place to try various Korean food then head over to guangzhang Market it's one of the oldest traditional markets in Seoul where you can try anything from bibimba Mandu Sunday and more it is very tourist heavy but extremely Foreigner friendly from my experience I found everything to be so affordable here as well next if you love amusement parks then check out latte world it's South Korea's version to Disneyland where there are many rides and attractions both indoor and outdoor indoor has more rides that are more kid-friendly while the outdoor air has more thrilling rides if you come be prepared to wait but I recommend to just get fast passes and tickets online in advance through Tracy Korea lastly you have to go Cafe hopping in Seoul there are so many trendy places in this city serving a variety of drinks and over-the-top pastries I love how some of the cafes are interactive too for example I was able to write a letter to myself which will be mailed to me one year later
Ground_Truth: Three things to do for your first time in Seoul, South Korea: If you want an all-in-one place to try various Korean food, then head over to Gwangjang Market. It's one of the oldest traditional markets in Seoul where you can try anything from bibimbap, mandu, soondae, and more. It is very tourist-heavy but extremely foreigner-friendly from my experience. I found everything to be so affordable here as well. Next, if you love amusement parks, then check out Lotte World. It's South Korea's version to Disneyland, where there are many rides and attractions both indoor and outdoor. Indoors have more rides that are more kid-friendly, while the outdoor area has more thrilling rides. If you come, be prepared to wait, but I recommend to just get fast passes and tickets online in advance to Trazy Korea. Lastly, you have to go cafe hopping in Seoul. There are so many trendy places in this city serving a variety of drinks and over-the-top pastries. I love how some of the cafes are interactive too. For example, I was able to write a letter to myself, which will be mailed to me one year later.

From my experience, I found everything to be so affordable here as well.   Next, if you love amusement parks, then check out Lotte World. youtube_caption: what is it like to visit China in 2023 well nothing like we thought it would be it was our first time back since covet and we thought with the current tensions between the west and China visiting Shanghai would be very different this year we couldn't have been more wrong as a tourist visiting China's biggest city there was virtually no difference from our last visit in 2018. in fact the people were even friendlier than we remembered and everyone welcomed us with smiles and politeness there is nothing we love more than proving our own preconceptions wrong and this is one of the main reasons we travel our short experience in Shanghai was the perfect example of that and that we should never let the way of places portrayed in the media shape our opinion of it
Ground_Truth: What is it like to visit China in 2023? Well, nothing like we thought it would be. It was our first time back since COVID, and we thought with the current tensions between the West and China, visiting Shanghai would be very different this year. We couldn't have been more wrong. As a tourist visiting China's biggest city, there was virtually no difference from our last visit in 2018. In fact, the people were even friendlier than we remembered, and everyone welcomed us with smiles and politeness. There is nothing we love more than proving our own preconceptions wrong, and this is one of the main reasons we travel. Our short experience in Shanghai was the perfect example of that, and we should never let the way a place is portrayed in the media shape our opinion of it.

youtube_caption: three things I wish I knew before my first trip to Cappadocia Turkey although the balloons fly most days they don't fly every day balloon flights are weather permitting so plan to visit for at least a few days in case of bad weather I was there two days and only got to see the balloons once due to rain depending on where you stay there might not be a Bank nearby the ATMs charge a hefty fee for taking out money so come prepared with euros and Turkish lira's already on hand it's a lot cheaper to exchange in Istanbul Cappadocia is so much more than just hot air balloons civilizations here have been living in caves for thousands of years you can explore entire underground cities cave dwellings fairy chimneys and the most unique Landscapes you'll ever see at Cappadocia to your bucket list and be sure to follow for more
Ground_Truth: Three things I wish I knew before my first trip to Cappadocia, Turkey. Although the balloons fly most days, they don't fly every day; balloon flights are weather permitting. So, plan to visit for at least a few days in case of bad weather. I was there for two days and only got to see the balloons once due to rain. Depending on where you stay, there might not be a bank nearby. ATMs charge a hefty fee for taking out money, so come prepared with euros and Turkish liras already on hand. It's a lot cheaper to exchange in Istanbul. Cappadocia is so much more than just hot air balloons. Civilizations here have been living in caves for thousands of years. {"title": "Cost of Japan trip for 14 days", "description": "", "top_comments": [{"comment": "That one person that said minimum 7-10,000 went nuts then💀"}, {"comment": "My mom is a flight attendant and flies to Japan often, she can usually bring a passenger with her for free if there are free seats on the flight. So in August I flew with her to Japan and  since she also gets a hotel room for free we basically had no spending, we were there for a week and bought basically everything we wanted and ended up spending around 700$ total. It was the best time of my life"}, {"comment": "Piece of Advice. Pay for the trip in advance for those who are worried about the cost. I usually buy my flight and stay 4-5 months out. By then, ive been paid enough times to recover from the cost of the trip. In addition, to those who have decent credit, take advantage of travel credit cards that pay mileage points. One of those reassurances so you're not completely breaking the bank for the trip. Once you're in Japan, a lot of the activities are relatively cheap"}, {"comment": "i’m 21 years old and went this summer. 

i found a flight to japan from washington DC and back for $1400 on canada air. split an air bnb with my friend so $15 a person for 3 weeks ($30 for the entire room without the split). food was less than $20 a day. we used train everywhere. it’s not too bad at all and it was awesome. ur never in your room anyway so why spend the money"}, {"comment": "I was in Japan for 27 days for about 2500-3000 euros

My BIGGEST TRICK. is to book your hotels on the same day you check in. they want to fill up and most hotels charge you like 50 bucks for the night and its Japan so the hotels are always clean and good!"}, {"comment": "She's more an anime character than some cosplays Ive seen."}, {"comment": "From Europe I spent less than 2k staying there one month in a guest house, and I saw A LOT of places, my base was in Tokyo but saw also Kamakura, Nara, Kyoto, Hakone and other places.
The flight was the biggest expense, still if you're smart you'll find out how to get better prices.
I also had a metro all line pass so I could get all the metro trains I wanted in Tokyo and went to Kyoto with a night bus, which was both cheap and allowed me to not waste a day.
Travel with brain, not with money...at least if you aren't rich."}, {"comment": "As someone who frequently travels to SE Asia, hearing someone say $20 a day for food is cheap blows my mind"}, {"comment": "Wouldn't the flight cost vary greatly based on where you're flying from?"}, {"comment": "I spent $1920 for flight and 2 week hotel stay in Hamamatsucho. $360ish on JR National Rail Pass for 2 weeks.(Unlimited).  Food was cheap. If you shop where the locals shop, shopping is reasonably priced. And this was during Golden Week."}, {"comment": "For anyone who’s held back only by the flight cost, I highly recommend flying Zipair. I grabbed a $95 dollar flight from NY to SFO then $700(!) round trip to Tokyo during a busier summer time. Way cheaper than the 1500-2000 you’ll pay elsewhere, and I can attest to zipairs service, food and airplanes."}, {"comment": "I was there for 24 days and spent a total of $4000 CAD ish. Includes flights ($1200) and train pass for the 21 days. Stayed in hostels and was traveling the main Kansai and Kanto areas"}, {"comment": "I go to Japan 2 times a year, spend an average of $1500 to $2000
If you go during the of season tix are like $500-$700 (from LAX)"}, {"comment": "She looks like Blake Lively.
The actress who did Gossip Girl etc etc"}, {"comment": "Japan is one place that I'll spend my vacation in."}, {"comment": "My sister and I spent £2000 for a month backpacking from Tokyo to Osaka and back. And we were splashing a bit too. Hostels in Japan are 100% the way to go (usually £15-30 a night and they're usually pretty snazzy), and convenience stores for food are a godsend."}, {"comment": "Spent 4000 for 5 weeks of japan and traveled the whole country. Its actually alot cheaper if u stay in hostels than what she refers to."}, {"comment": "My trip to japan ends with me going to akihabara"}, {"comment": "Went to Japan with $2800 Usd for 17 days, as long as you know where to find cheap flights, accomodations and food, you're gold!"}, {"comment": "Literally the only thing stopping me is the flight ticket cost."}]}
